text.title: BA Week-One Onboarding Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every Week-One pack must satisfy. Each rule is enforced by the Day-5 validator; failure means the BA is not yet "ready to write requirements."

rule.statement: Day 1 produces a flat inventory of every existing project artefact (file, ticket, channel, board, repo) with: source URL, last-modified date, and a 10-word summary. The inventory is the single source for the rest of the week — interviews never replace it.

rationale.source: BABOK §3.1.4 Plan Business Analysis Information Management

rule.rationale: Skipping the artefact pull leads to BAs re-asking what is already written, which burns stakeholder trust and surfaces only what stakeholders remember (recency-biased).

rule.statement: The stakeholders.md file MUST list each stakeholder with: full name, role, decision authority (Approve / Consult / Inform / None), preferred channel, and a one-line "what they care about." A stakeholder marked "TBD" or "the client team" is a failure.

rationale.source: BABOK §10.43 Stakeholder List, Map, or Personas

rule.rationale: Anonymous stakeholder groups produce vague requirements; named owners produce decisions. RACI-like authority labels prevent the BA from chasing sign-off from someone who cannot grant it.

rule.statement: Day 3 produces glossary.md with >= 15 domain terms. Each entry has: term, definition, source (interview/document/observed usage), and a flag if the definition was contested between stakeholders.
rule.rationale: Conflicting term usage is the highest-frequency source of late-stage requirements rework. A written glossary forces the conflict into the open during week one, where it is cheap to resolve.

rule.statement: Day 4 produces processes.md containing at least one BPMN-style or swimlane diagram of the as-is core business process. Pure text descriptions are not acceptable. The diagram must label each lane with a named owner from stakeholders.md.
rule.rationale: A diagram exposes handoff points that a prose description hides. Tying lanes to stakeholders also reveals process ownership gaps (lanes nobody owns) before requirements work begins.

rule.statement: Day 5 produces risks.md with: top 5 known gaps (info missing for requirements), top 5 risks (with likelihood + impact 1-3), and explicit "open questions for the PM" with owner and required-by date. The memo is signed off by the BA's engagement manager in writing (PR comment, email, Slack ack with timestamp).
rule.rationale: Projects without a documented gap memo at start consistently underestimate discovery scope. The signed gap memo also protects the BA against scope creep allegations later: "this risk was logged on Day 5."

example.title: Stakeholder line that passes r2
example.code: | Name | Role | Authority | Channel | Cares about | |------|------|-----------|---------|-------------| | Maria Lopes | VP Operations | Approve | Email + Tue 10:00 sync | Reducing manual order entry by 50% |
example.note: Named, authority labelled, single concrete care-about. The BA can now route a scope question to a real human with known authority.

example.title: Stakeholder line that fails r2
example.code: | Name | Role | Authority | Channel | Cares about | |------|------|-----------|---------|-------------| | Client Ops Team | TBD | TBD | TBD | TBD |
example.note: Three "TBD" + group name. The BA has effectively no stakeholder for the most critical role. The pack must reject this row.
